an9.36:1.1: “*Bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the first *jhāna*;
an9.36:1.2: *bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the second *jhāna*;
an9.36:1.3: *bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the third *jhāna*;
an9.36:1.4: *bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the fourth *jhāna*;
an9.36:1.5: *bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*];
an9.36:1.6: *bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*];
an9.36:1.7: *bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*];
an9.36:1.8: *bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the dimension of neither-*saññā*-nor-non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*];
an9.36:1.9: *bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*].
an9.36:10.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, as far as attainment with *saññā* [*saññāsamāpatti*] extends, so far extends penetration to final knowledge [*aññāpaṭivedho*].
an9.36:10.2: And as for these two dimensions [*āyatanāni*], *bhikkhus*, to be relied on—
an9.36:10.3: the attainment of the dimension of neither-*saññā*-nor-non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanasamāpatti*] and the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodho*]—I say that these are to be rightly declared by meditators [*jhāyīhi*] skilled in attainments and skilled in emerging from attainments, after entering them and emerging from them.”
an9.36:2.1: “‘*Bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the first *jhāna*’—this is what was said.
an9.36:2.2: Because of what was this said?
an9.36:2.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*.
an9.36:2.4: Whatever there is there of form, of *vedanā*, of *saññā*, of *saṅkhāras*, of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], he regards those *dhammas* as impermanent [*aniccā*], as *dukkha*, as a disease, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as an affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self [*anattā*].
an9.36:2.5: He draws the mind [*citta*] back from those *dhammas*.
an9.36:2.6: Having drawn the mind [*citta*] back from those *dhammas*, he brings the mind [*citta*] to the deathless element [*amatāya dhātuyā*]:
an9.36:2.7: ‘This is peaceful, this is sublime: that is, the settling of all *saṅkhāras* [*sabbasaṅkhārasamatho*], the relinquishment of all acquisitions [*sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo*], the ending of *taṇhā* [*taṇhākkhayo*], fading away [*virāgo*], cessation [*nirodho*], *nibbāna*.’
an9.36:2.8: Established there, he attains the destruction of the *āsavas*.
an9.36:2.9: If he does not attain the destruction of the *āsavas*, then through that very passion for *dhamma* [*dhammarāga*], through that delight in *dhamma* [*dhammanandiyā*], with the exhaustion of the five lower fetters, he becomes one who is spontaneously reborn; there he attains final *nibbāna*, of a nature not to return from that world.
an9.36:3.1: Just as, *bhikkhus*, an archer or an archer’s apprentice, having made a straw dummy or a clay mound into a practice target, after some time becomes one who shoots far, pierces a tiny mark, and splits a great mass;
an9.36:3.10: that is what was said, and it was said because of this.
an9.36:3.2: even so, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures … enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*.
an9.36:3.3: Whatever there is there of form, of *vedanā*, of *saññā*, of *saṅkhāras*, of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], he regards those *dhammas* as impermanent [*aniccā*], as *dukkha*, as a disease, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as an affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self [*anattā*].
an9.36:3.4: He draws the mind [*citta*] back from those *dhammas*.
an9.36:3.5: Having drawn the mind [*citta*] back from those *dhammas*, he brings the mind [*citta*] to the deathless element [*amatāya dhātuyā*]:
an9.36:3.6: ‘This is peaceful, this is sublime: that is, the settling of all *saṅkhāras* [*sabbasaṅkhārasamatho*], the relinquishment of all acquisitions [*sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo*], the ending of *taṇhā* [*taṇhākkhayo*], fading away [*virāgo*], cessation [*nirodho*], *nibbāna*.’
an9.36:3.7: Established there, he attains the destruction of the *āsavas*.
an9.36:3.8: If he does not attain the destruction of the *āsavas*, then through that very passion for *dhamma* [*dhammarāga*], through that delight in *dhamma* [*dhammanandiyā*], with the exhaustion of the five lower fetters, he becomes one who is spontaneously reborn; there he attains final *nibbāna*, of a nature not to return from that world.
an9.36:3.9: ‘*Bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the first *jhāna*’—
an9.36:4.1: *Bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the second *jhāna* …
an9.36:4.10: Established there, he attains the destruction of the *āsavas*.
an9.36:4.11: If he does not attain the destruction of the *āsavas*, then through that very passion for *dhamma* [*dhammarāga*], through that delight in *dhamma* [*dhammanandiyā*], with the exhaustion of the five lower fetters, he becomes one who is spontaneously reborn; there he attains final *nibbāna*, of a nature not to return from that world.
an9.36:4.2: *bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the third *jhāna* …
an9.36:4.3: ‘*Bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the fourth *jhāna*’—this is what was said.
an9.36:4.4: Because of what was this said?
an9.36:4.5: Here, *bhikkhus*, with the giving up of *sukha* and the giving up of *dukkha*, and with the earlier vanishing of joy and distress, a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither painful nor pleasant and is purified by equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati*.
an9.36:4.6: Whatever there is there of form, of *vedanā*, of *saññā*, of *saṅkhāras*, of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], he regards those *dhammas* as impermanent [*aniccā*], as *dukkha*, as a disease, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as an affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self [*anattā*].
an9.36:4.7: He draws the mind [*citta*] back from those *dhammas*.
an9.36:4.8: Having drawn the mind [*citta*] back from those *dhammas*, he brings the mind [*citta*] to the deathless element [*amatāya dhātuyā*]:
an9.36:4.9: ‘This is peaceful, this is sublime: that is, the settling of all *saṅkhāras* [*sabbasaṅkhārasamatho*], the relinquishment of all acquisitions [*sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo*], the ending of *taṇhā* [*taṇhākkhayo*], fading away [*virāgo*], cessation [*nirodho*], *nibbāna*.’
an9.36:5.1: Just as, *bhikkhus*, an archer or an archer’s apprentice, having made a straw dummy or a clay mound into a practice target, after some time becomes one who shoots far, pierces a tiny mark, and splits a great mass;
an9.36:5.2: even so, *bhikkhus*, with the giving up of *sukha* … a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*.
an9.36:5.3: Whatever there is there of form, of *vedanā* …
an9.36:5.4: of a nature not to return from that world.
an9.36:5.5: ‘*Bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the fourth *jhāna*’—that is what was said, and it was said because of this.
an9.36:6.1: ‘*Bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*]’—this is what was said.
an9.36:6.2: Because of what was this said?
an9.36:6.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, with the complete transcending of form-*saññās* [*rūpasaññānaṁ*], with the vanishing of resistance-*saññās* [*paṭighasaññānaṁ*], through non-attention to diversity-*saññās* [*nānattasaññānaṁ*], thinking, ‘Space is infinite,’ a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*].
an9.36:6.4: Whatever there is there of *vedanā*, of *saññā*, of *saṅkhāras*, of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], he regards those *dhammas* as impermanent [*aniccā*], as *dukkha*, as a disease, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as an affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self [*anattā*].
an9.36:6.5: He draws the mind [*citta*] back from those *dhammas*.
an9.36:6.6: Having drawn the mind [*citta*] back from those *dhammas*, he brings the mind [*citta*] to the deathless element [*amatāya dhātuyā*]:
an9.36:6.7: ‘This is peaceful, this is sublime: that is, the settling of all *saṅkhāras* [*sabbasaṅkhārasamatho*], the relinquishment of all acquisitions [*sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo*], the ending of *taṇhā* [*taṇhākkhayo*], fading away [*virāgo*], cessation [*nirodho*], *nibbāna*.’
an9.36:6.8: Established there, he attains the destruction of the *āsavas*.
an9.36:6.9: If he does not attain the destruction of the *āsavas*, then through that very passion for *dhamma* [*dhammarāga*], through that delight in *dhamma* [*dhammanandiyā*], with the exhaustion of the five lower fetters, he becomes one who is spontaneously reborn; there he attains final *nibbāna*, of a nature not to return from that world.
an9.36:7.1: Just as, *bhikkhus*, an archer or an archer’s apprentice, having made a straw dummy or a clay mound into a practice target, after some time becomes one who shoots far, pierces a tiny mark, and splits a great mass;
an9.36:7.2: even so, *bhikkhus*, with the complete transcending of form-*saññās* [*rūpasaññānaṁ*], with the vanishing of resistance-*saññās* [*paṭighasaññānaṁ*], through non-attention to diversity-*saññās* [*nānattasaññānaṁ*], thinking, ‘Space is infinite,’ a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*].
an9.36:7.3: Whatever there is there of *vedanā*, of *saññā* …
an9.36:7.4: of a nature not to return from that world.
an9.36:7.5: ‘*Bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*]’—
an9.36:7.6: that is what was said, and it was said because of this.
an9.36:8.1: ‘*Bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*] …
an9.36:8.10: If he does not attain the destruction of the *āsavas*, then through that very passion for *dhamma* [*dhammarāga*], through that delight in *dhamma* [*dhammanandiyā*], with the exhaustion of the five lower fetters, he becomes one who is spontaneously reborn; there he attains final *nibbāna*, of a nature not to return from that world.
an9.36:8.2: *bhikkhus*, I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*]’—this is what was said.
an9.36:8.3: Because of what was this said?
an9.36:8.4: Here, *bhikkhus*, having completely gone beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘There is nothing,’ a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*].
an9.36:8.5: Whatever there is there of *vedanā*, of *saññā*, of *saṅkhāras*, of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], he regards those *dhammas* as impermanent [*aniccā*], as *dukkha*, as a disease, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as an affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self [*anattā*].
an9.36:8.6: He draws the mind [*citta*] back from those *dhammas*.
an9.36:8.7: Having drawn the mind [*citta*] back from those *dhammas*, he brings the mind [*citta*] to the deathless element [*amatāya dhātuyā*]:
an9.36:8.8: ‘This is peaceful, this is sublime: that is, the settling of all *saṅkhāras* [*sabbasaṅkhārasamatho*], the relinquishment of all acquisitions [*sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo*], the ending of *taṇhā* [*taṇhākkhayo*], fading away [*virāgo*], cessation [*nirodho*], *nibbāna*.’
an9.36:8.9: Established there, he attains the destruction of the *āsavas*.
an9.36:9.1: Just as, *bhikkhus*, an archer or an archer’s apprentice, having made a straw dummy or a clay mound into a practice target, after some time becomes one who shoots far, pierces a tiny mark, and splits a great mass;
an9.36:9.10: that is what was said, and it was said because of this.
an9.36:9.2: even so, *bhikkhus*, having completely gone beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘There is nothing,’ a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*].
an9.36:9.3: Whatever there is there of *vedanā*, of *saññā*, of *saṅkhāras*, of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], he regards those *dhammas* as impermanent [*aniccā*], as *dukkha*, as a disease, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as an affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self [*anattā*].
an9.36:9.4: He draws the mind [*citta*] back from those *dhammas*.
an9.36:9.5: Having drawn the mind [*citta*] back from those *dhammas*, he brings the mind [*citta*] to the deathless element [*amatāya dhātuyā*]:
an9.36:9.6: ‘This is peaceful, this is sublime: that is, the settling of all *saṅkhāras* [*sabbasaṅkhārasamatho*], the relinquishment of all acquisitions [*sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo*], the ending of *taṇhā* [*taṇhākkhayo*], fading away [*virāgo*], cessation [*nirodho*], *nibbāna*.’
an9.36:9.7: Established there, he attains the destruction of the *āsavas*.
an9.36:9.8: If he does not attain the destruction of the *āsavas*, then through that very passion for *dhamma* [*dhammarāga*], through that delight in *dhamma* [*dhammanandiyā*], with the exhaustion of the five lower fetters, he becomes one who is spontaneously reborn; there he attains final *nibbāna*, of a nature not to return from that world.
an9.36:9.9: ‘I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* comes by relying on the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*]’—
